title: LLM Architecture — Transformers, Scaling Laws & Training
description: A decoder-only Transformer (GPT-style) is the dominant LLM architecture. At FAANG Staff level, you must understand every design knob: attention variants, positional encoding, normalization placement, FFN gating, and how these choices affect training stability, inference efficiency, and quality at scale.

sections.title: The Transformer Decoder: Anatomy of a Block
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: A decoder-only block takes N vectors (one per token) and outputs N refined vectors. Each block has two sub-layers: Multi-Head Self-Attention (tokens look at each other) and Feed-Forward Network (each token thinks independently). Both are wrapped with residual connections and normalization.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Llama 3 8B has 32 decoder blocks. Each block: QKV projection (3 × 4096 × 4096), attention output projection (4096 × 4096), FFN gate+up+down (3 × 4096 × 14336). Total per-block params ≈ 28M. Total model = 32 × 28M + embeddings + LM head ≈ 8B.

track_changes Practical Workflow: For a new architecture, start with the d_model dimension (typically 4096 for 8B, 8192 for 70B). Scale n_layers proportionally. Use d_ff = 8/3 × d_model for SwiGLU (standard practice).

search Interview Angle: "Design a 70B parameter decoder-only transformer." Walk through: d_model=8192, n_layers=80, n_heads=64, n_kv_heads=8, d_ff=28672 (SwiGLU). Explain why 80 layers instead of 64 (depth helps reasoning). Mention you'd use pre-RMSNorm for training stability.
sections.codeBlock:
# Decoder-only block (simplified)
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F

class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, eps=1e-6):
        super().__init__()
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(dim))
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        rms = x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True).sqrt() + self.eps
        return x / rms * self.weight

class DecoderBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_heads, d_ff):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, n_heads, batch_first=True)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=False),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model, bias=False),
        )
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(d_model)
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x, mask=None):
        # Pre-norm: norm before sublayer (standard for modern LLMs)
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x), self.norm1(x), self.norm1(x),
                          attn_mask=mask, need_weights=False)[0]
        x = x + self.ffn(self.norm2(x))
        return x

sections.title: Attention Mechanisms: MHA → MQA → GQA → MLA
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The attention mechanism computes how much each token should 'attend' to every other token. The bottleneck is the Key-Value cache — storing K and V for every token at every layer. Different attention variants trade off quality vs KV cache size.

Evolution: MHA (one K,V per head) → MQA (all heads share one K,V) → GQA (heads share within groups) → MLA (compressed latent K,V). Each step reduces KV cache memory by 4-16×.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Llama 3 8B: H=32 query heads, G=8 KV heads → 4× KV cache reduction. Memory for 128K context: MHA would need ~64GB, GQA needs ~16GB. DeepSeek V2's MLA compresses KV to 1/10 of GQA by projecting to a low-dimensional latent space.

search Interview Angle: "Which attention variant for a 1M context model?" Propose MLA + RingAttention + FlashAttention-3. MLA for KV cache (1B tokens × 80 layers × 2 bytes = 160GB cached tokens, MLA compresses to ~16GB). RingAttention for distributing sequence across GPUs. FlashAttention-3 for FP8 attention computation.
sections.codeBlock:
# KV cache memory comparison at 128K context, 80 layers
import math

def kv_cache_gb(d_model, n_layers, n_kv_heads, context_len, bytes_per=2):
    kv_per_head = d_model // max(1, n_kv_heads) if n_kv_heads else 0
    if n_kv_heads == 0:  # MLA case
        # MLA: compressed latent = d_model // 8
        latent_dim = d_model // 8
        return 2 * n_layers * context_len * latent_dim * bytes_per / 1e9
    return 2 * n_layers * context_len * n_kv_heads * kv_per_head * bytes_per / 1e9

# Compare at 70B scale (d_model=8192)
print(f"MHA  (H=64, KV=64): {kv_cache_gb(8192, 80, 64, 131072):.1f} GB")
print(f"MQA  (H=64, KV=1):  {kv_cache_gb(8192, 80, 1, 131072):.1f} GB")
print(f"GQA  (H=64, KV=8):  {kv_cache_gb(8192, 80, 8, 131072):.1f} GB")
print(f"MLA  (compressed):  {kv_cache_gb(8192, 80, 0, 131072):.1f} GB")

# FAANG Q: "If MQA is 64× smaller cache, why use GQA?"
# A: MQA hurts quality at large scale — GQA matches MHA quality
# with 8× fewer KV heads. Llama 3 uses GQA at all model scales.

sections.title: Positional Encoding: RoPE, ALiBi, YaRN, NTK
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Self-attention is permutation-invariant — it doesn't know token order. Positional encoding adds order information. RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) is the modern standard because it encodes relative position naturally through rotation.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: RoPE at 128K context length: θ=10000, base frequency = 1/10000^(2i/d). NTK-aware scaling can extend RoPE to 4× training length without fine-tuning by adjusting the base frequency. Llama 3 405B uses NTK-aware scaling to reach 128K context from 8K pre-training length.

track_changes Practical Workflow: To extend context: 1) Try NTK-aware with adjusted base frequency. 2) If that's not enough, add YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN) which applies frequency-specific scaling. 3) For extreme extension (>4×), fine-tune with position interpolation.
sections.codeBlock:
# RoPE implementation (core rotation)
def apply_rotary_emb(xq, xk, pos, theta=10000.0):
    B, H, T, D = xq.shape
    # Compute frequencies for each dimension pair
    freqs = 1.0 / (theta ** (torch.arange(0, D, 2).float() / D))
    freqs = pos.unsqueeze(-1) * freqs.unsqueeze(0)  # [T, D/2]
    cos = freqs.cos()
    sin = freqs.sin()

    # Rotate half-dimensions
    def rotate(x):
        x_rot = x.clone()
        x_rot[..., ::2] = x[..., ::2] * cos - x[..., 1::2] * sin
        x_rot[..., 1::2] = x[..., ::2] * sin + x[..., 1::2] * cos
        return x_rot
    return rotate(xq), rotate(xk)

# NTK-aware scaling: adjust theta for length extension
def ntk_scale_theta(base_theta, orig_len, target_len, scale=0.5):
    # Higher theta = higher frequency = better long-range attention
    factor = (target_len / orig_len) ** scale
    return base_theta * factor

print(f"Original θ: {10000.0:.0f}")
print(f"NTK 8K→32K: {ntk_scale_theta(10000.0, 8192, 32768):.0f}")
print(f"NTK 8K→128K: {ntk_scale_theta(10000.0, 8192, 131072):.0f}")

sections.title: FFN Variants: ReLU, GELU, SiLU, SwiGLU
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The FFN layer is where the model 'thinks' — each token independently transforms its representation through a wider hidden dimension. The gating mechanism (GLU variants) lets each token learn whether to activate its FFN pathway.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: SwiGLU (used in Llama 3, PaLM, Gemma) uses a gated mechanism: output = (SiLU(xW_gate) ⊙ xW_up) × W_down. Requires 3 weight matrices vs 2 for standard ReLU FFN. At same param count, SwiGLU needs 2/3 the hidden dimension of ReLU for equivalent perplexity. Llama 3 8B: d_ff=14336 (vs 11008 for Llama 2's ReLU).

search Interview Angle: "Why SwiGLU over ReLU?" SwiGLU has non-zero gradient for negative inputs (no dying ReLU), enables better gradient flow in deep networks, and empirically achieves lower perplexity at same compute budget. The 50% more matrix multiplications is worth the quality gain.

sections.title: Scaling Laws Deep Dive — Beyond Chinchilla
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Scaling laws tell you how much model size, data, and compute you need for optimal perplexity. Chinchilla says 20 tokens/param. But Llama 3 trained on 15T tokens for an 8B model (1075 tokens/param) — why?

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Chinchilla (2022): optimal = 20 tokens/param. Llama 3 (2024): 8B model, 15T tokens = 1875 tokens/param. The difference: Chinchilla optimized for perplexity at a fixed compute budget. Llama 3 optimized for downstream performance at the same model size — more data doesn't hurt quality, only training cost. Key insight: perplexity keeps decreasing with more data even beyond Chinchilla-optimal, and the marginal gain on downstream tasks remains positive.

track_changes Practical Workflow: Given a compute budget C: 1) Use Chinchilla to find initial N and D. 2) Run small-scale experiments (C/1000 budget) to find optimal N:D ratio for your actual downstream metrics. 3) Scale up, monitoring saturation curves.

search Interview Angle: "You have $10M to train an LLM. How big and how much data?" Frame answer with: $10M ≈ 500K A100-hours. At Chinchilla: 7B model, 140B tokens. But you should consider: training is 80% of cost, inference is 20%. If deployed widely, prefer under-trained 7B (faster inference) over optimally-trained 13B.
sections.codeBlock:
# Compute budget estimation
def chinchilla_optimal(compute_flops):
    """Estimate optimal N and D given compute budget in FLOP."""
    # Chinchilla: N_opt ∝ C^0.5, D_opt ∝ C^0.5
    # Reference: 70B on 1.4T tokens ≈ 1e24 FLOP
    ref_flops = 1e24
    ref_params = 70e9
    ref_tokens = 1.4e12

    scale = (compute_flops / ref_flops) ** 0.5
    return ref_params * scale, ref_tokens * scale

budgets = [1e22, 1e23, 1e24, 1e25]  # FLOP
for b in budgets:
    N, D = chinchilla_optimal(b)
    print(f"C={b:.0e}: N={N/1e9:.1f}B, D={D/1e12:.2f}T tokens")

# Real budget for Llama 3 8B: ~1e23 FLOP
# → Chinchilla optimal: 22B model, 440B tokens
# → Actual: 8B model, 15T tokens
# FAANG staff question: why the gap?
# Answer: Chinchilla assumed fixed FLOP budget with no inference cost.
# For models deployed at scale (like Llama 3), smaller models with
# more data are cheaper to serve, even if training cost is higher.

sections.title: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm & RMSNorm
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Normalization prevents activations from growing/shrinking across layers. Post-norm (original Transformer, GPT-2): LayerNorm after residual addition. Pre-norm (GPT-3, all modern LLMs): LayerNorm before each sublayer. RMSNorm (Llama): Simplified LayerNorm — removes mean-centering, just normalizes by RMS. ~15% faster.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Pre-norm enables training 2-3× deeper models than post-norm at same stability. RMSNorm saves ~15% on normalization FLOPs (minor, but every percent matters at 70B+ scale). Llama 3 uses pre-RMSNorm exclusively.

search Interview Angle: "Why does pre-norm outperform post-norm for deep transformers?" The key is gradient flow: with pre-norm, the residual path (x = x + F(x)) doesn't pass through normalization, giving gradients a clean highway. With post-norm, gradients must flow through LayerNorm's scale/shift parameters, which can arbitrarily gate the gradient. For 50+ layer models, pre-norm makes the difference between converging and diverging.
sections.codeBlock:
# Post-norm vs pre-norm gradient flow
import torch, torch.nn as nn

def post_norm_block(x, sublayer, norm):
    """Post-norm: LayerNorm after residual."""
    return norm(x + sublayer(x))
    # Gradient flows through: ∂norm/∂(x+sublayer(x)) * (1 + ∂sublayer/∂x)
    # norm has learnable scale/shift that can compress gradients

def pre_norm_block(x, sublayer, norm):
    """Pre-norm: LayerNorm before sublayer."""
    return x + sublayer(norm(x))
    # Gradient flows: 1 + ∂sublayer/∂x * ∂norm/∂x
    # Identity path has no gates — gradients flow unobstructed

# RMSNorm vs LayerNorm: ~15% faster
# LayerNorm: E[x] = 0, Var[x] = 1. RMSNorm: ||x|| / sqrt(d)
# LayerNorm computes mean + variance (2 reductions).
# RMSNorm computes mean of squares (1 reduction).

sections.title: Vocabulary Design & Tokenizer Selection
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The tokenizer partitions input text into vocabulary tokens. It determines how many tokens each input costs, how many languages the model supports, and how robust it is to misspellings. At 50M+ queries/day, a 10% tokenizer efficiency difference = millions in savings.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Llama 3: 128K vocabulary, tiktoken BPE (same as GPT-4). Llama 2 had 32K vocabulary, SentencePiece. The 4× increase in vocab size means: +128K × 4096 embedding dim = ~500M extra parameters in the embedding + LM head. But each token is 1.5-2× more information-dense, so effective context length increases by 50% for the same KV cache. English token ratio: ~1.2 tokens/word for Llama 3 vs ~1.8 for Llama 2.

track_changes Practical Workflow: Evaluate tokenizer on: compression ratio across target languages, special token count (reserve IDs 0-255 for byte-fallback), UTF-8 handling, number tokenization (should NOT split '42' into '4' + '2').
sections.codeBlock:
# Tokenizer evaluation
import tiktoken

def evaluate_tokenizer(encoding_name, texts):
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding(encoding_name)
    total_tokens = sum(len(enc.encode(t)) for t in texts)
    total_chars = sum(len(t) for t in texts)
    print(f"{encoding_name}: {total_tokens} tokens, "
          f"{total_chars / max(1, total_tokens):.2f} char/token")

samples = [
    "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.",
    "123456789",
    "你好，世界！",
    "def fibonacci(n): return n if n <= 1 else fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)"
]

evaluate_tokenizer("cl100k_base", samples)  # GPT-4 / Llama 3
# Notice: Chinese text uses ~3× more tokens than English
# Numbers may be split unexpectedly: '123' → ['12', '3']
# ponytail: real eval measures token/word ratio across 100+ languages

sections.title: Context Window Architecture: Ring Attention & Block-Sparse
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Full attention scales O(N²) with context length. For 1M+ tokens, this is computationally infeasible without specialized techniques. Solutions: Ring Attention (distribute sequence across GPUs, each processes a chunk, communicate in a ring), Block-Sparse Attention (skip attending to irrelevant blocks), FlashAttention (tiled computation, no full attention matrix in HBM).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: 128K context, 70B model: standard attention needs ~256KB per token × 128K = ~32GB attention matrix (FP16). FlashAttention reduces this to ~2GB via tiling. Ring Attention with 8 GPUs: each GPU processes 16K tokens, communicates results around a ring. Total compute: O(N²/G) per GPU.

search Interview Angle: "Design a model with 10M context." At 10M tokens: O(N²) is impossible even with FlashAttention. Propose: Block-Sparse attention (local attention to neighbors, global attention to learned anchor tokens every 1024 positions), combined with RingAttention over 64+ GPUs, and MQA for KV cache efficiency. Google's Gemini 1.5 Pro uses a variant of this (Mixture of Attention heads with different sparsity patterns).
sections.codeBlock:
# FlashAttention: tiled attention computation (conceptual)
def flash_attention_tile(Q, K, V, block_size=128):
    """
    Standard attention computes S=QK^T (N² memory).
    FlashAttention processes in blocks, never materializing full S.
    """
    B, H, N, D = Q.shape
    output = torch.zeros_like(Q)
    for i in range(0, N, block_size):
        q_i = Q[:, :, i:i+block_size]  # [B, H, block, D]
        for j in range(0, N, block_size):
            k_j = K[:, :, j:j+block_size]  # [B, H, block, D]
            v_j = V[:, :, j:j+block_size]
            # On-chip SRAM: ~128KB per block (vs GB in HBM)
            s_ij = q_i @ k_j.transpose(-2, -1) / math.sqrt(D)
            # Online softmax scaling (rescales on the fly)
            # ... (simplified — real impl uses separate max/sum)
    return output
# ponytail: real FlashAttention-3 uses FP8 matmul on Blackwell GPUs,
# achieving ~1 exaFLOP in attention for 128K context

sections.title: Architecture Decision Tree: Dense vs MoE vs Hybrid
sections.description:
track_changes Practical Workflow: When designing an LLM architecture, use this decision tree:

1. Budget & deployment constraints: Is inference cost or quality the primary constraint? If inference cost (serving billions of queries/day), consider MoE (3-5× better quality/active-param ratio). If quality first with moderate serving, go dense.

2. Memory budget: Can you fit all parameters in VRAM? 70B dense = 140GB (FP16). 70B MoE with 8 experts = 140GB total, 25GB active. If VRAM limited, MoE gives more active compute per GB.

3. Batch size & throughput: MoE excels at small batch sizes (chat) where active params are utilized. At very large batch sizes (>1024 sequences), all-to-all communication overhead erodes the advantage. Dense models are more predictable at scale.

4. Hybrid approach: Use dense for early layers (which learn universal features), MoE for later layers (which specialize). DeepSeek V2 uses a hybrid approach: 3 dense + 55 MoE layers.

bar_chart Concrete comparison: Mixtral 8x7B (47B total, 13B active) ≈ Llama 2 70B quality at 5× lower active params → ~3× higher inference throughput. But uses 2× more total VRAM.

search Staff Engineer Question: "You need to serve 100M queries/day with <200ms latency. Choose dense 70B or MoE 8x7B." Answer: MoE 8x7B. At batch size 1 (typical for query serving), MoE's 13B active params give 5× less compute per token than 70B dense. You can fit 3× more MoE instances per GPU. The all-to-all overhead is negligible at single-batch inference. Total GPU cost: ~$2M/month for MoE vs ~$6M for dense 70B.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017) — The original Transformer paper
  • Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla, 2022) — Scaling laws for LLM compute optimization
  • Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models — Industry reference architecture with GQA + RoPE + pre-RMSNorm
  • Llama 3 Herd of Models — Latest Llama: 15T tokens, 128K context, 128K vocabulary, GQA at all scales
  • DeepNet: Scaling Transformers to 1,000 Layers — DeepNorm initialization for ultra-deep transformers
  • NormFormer: Improved Transformer Training with Extra Normalization — LayerNorm placement study

details:
Prerequisite: Understand the Transformer Architecture (original encoder-decoder) before diving into decoder-only variants. Also review Neural Network Training for optimization fundamentals.

Key Architecture Specs — Llama 3 Family:

Param8B70B405B
d_model4096819216384
n_layers3280126
n_heads3264128
n_kv_heads888
d_ff (SwiGLU)143362867253248
vocab_size128K128K128K
activationSwiGLUSwiGLUSwiGLU
normRMSNormRMSNormRMSNorm
rope_theta500K500K500K
lr (max)3e-41.5e-48e-5
batch_size4M4M4M
Context128K128K128K

Staff Engineer tip: When designing a new LLM, the 4 critical architecture knobs are: d_model ratio (width:depth = ~2:1 to ~4:1), FFN intermediate size (8/3 × d_model for SwiGLU), KV head ratio (8:1 for GQA is standard), and vocabulary size (128K for general-purpose, 32K for domain-specific). Always start with Llama 3's proven ratios and adjust from there.
